title: Capstone: Build the Planner System
concept: Assembling a hybrid planner that dynamically switches from ReAct to backtracking based on difficulty; 'Next up: Tool Use in Agentic Loops.'.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: what does an agent do when its steps, tokens, or time are strictly capped?

options.text: It plans carefully to get the most done within the limit
It ignores the limit and keeps going forever
It always picks the slowest, most expensive path

exercises.explain: Under resource limits, a smart agent budgets its moves, choosing the plan that reaches the goal without wasting steps, tokens, or time.

cards.title: The Grand Finale
cards.body: You've learned ReAct, backtracking, and planning under limits. Now let's snap them together into one smart planner that picks the right approach on its own.

cards.title: What Is a Hybrid Planner?
cards.body: A hybrid planner is an agent that can switch between planning styles. For easy tasks it uses fast ReAct (think-then-act), and for hard, tricky tasks it uses backtracking (try, and undo dead ends).

cards.title: Like a Smart Car Gearbox
cards.body: On a flat road the car cruises in high gear for speed. On a steep hill it drops to low gear for power. A hybrid planner shifts 'gears' based on how tough the task is.

cards.title: Seeing It Work
cards.body: Ask 'What's 2+2?' and the planner uses quick ReAct. Ask 'Plan a 3-city trip under a tight budget with closed roads' and it switches to backtracking to test and undo bad routes.

cards.title: Myth: One Method Fits All
cards.body: Some think the 'best' planner just uses backtracking for everything. But that's slow and wasteful on easy tasks. The real skill is matching the method to the difficulty.

cards.title: Two Modes, One Brain
cards.body: The planner reads the task difficulty first, then routes to the right mode.

left.text: Uses ReAct: think a step, act, repeat. Fast and cheap.

right.text: Uses backtracking: try paths, undo dead ends, find a valid plan.

exercises.prompt: A hybrid planner gets a hard puzzle with many dead-end paths. Which mode should it switch to?

options.text: Backtracking, so it can undo wrong moves and try new paths
Fast ReAct, because speed matters most here
It should give up since the task is hard
It should answer randomly to save time

exercises.explain: Hard tasks with dead ends need backtracking, which lets the agent undo bad choices and explore other paths until it finds one that works.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A hybrid planner decides which method to use based on how difficult the task is.
exercises.explain: That's the whole point of a hybrid planner: it checks the difficulty first, then dynamically switches between ReAct and backtracking.

exercises.sentence: For a hard, dead-end-filled task, the planner switches to backtracking to undo bad choices.
exercises.explain: Backtracking is the mode for hard tasks because it can step back from dead ends and try other paths.

exercises.prompt: Which response was written by an AI planner explaining how it chose its mode?

options.text: This task looks simple, so I'll use ReAct: I'll think one step, act, and repeat until done.
I refuse to plan and will not switch modes for any task.

exercises.explain: The AI planner clearly states the task's difficulty and names the matching mode (ReAct) with its reasoning, which is how a hybrid planner explains its choice.

exercises.prompt: You build a hybrid planner. It gets an easy question, then a hard multi-step puzzle with tight token limits. What should it do across both?

options.text: Use fast ReAct on the easy one, switch to budget-aware backtracking on the hard one
Use slow backtracking on both, even the easy question
Use fast ReAct on both, even the tricky dead-end puzzle
Ignore difficulty and pick a random mode each time

exercises.explain: A great hybrid planner reads difficulty and limits, then routes each task to the right mode: ReAct for easy and cheap, backtracking for hard, all while respecting the token budget. That's assembling everything you've learned into one smart system. Next up: Tool Use in Agentic Loops.
